title: Urinary catheter insertion
executiveSummary: Insert a urinary catheter only with a justified indication, using sterile technique, appropriate gauge, secure fixation, and a closed system to prevent catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI).
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Verify indication and contraindications; confirm patient identity and prescription (type, gauge, expected duration).
2. Perform hand hygiene and prepare the sterile kit on a clean surface.
3. Position the patient: supine, knees semi-flexed; separate the labia in females; elevate the penis to 90° in males.
4. Disinfect the urethral meatus with antiseptic in a circular motion from center to periphery; allow to dry if indicated by protocol.
5. Insert the catheter using sterile technique: lubricate and gently introduce without forcing until clear urine return is observed.
6. Inflate the balloon with the indicated volume (usually 10 mL of sterile water; follow manufacturer instructions).
7. Connect to a drainage bag in a **closed system**; avoid unnecessary disconnections.
8. Secure the catheter without tension (thigh for females, suprapubic abdomen for males).
9. Position the drainage bag below the level of the bladder; do not rest it on the floor.
10. Document the time, gauge, balloon volume, initial urine appearance, and person responsible.
11. Re-evaluate the catheter indication every shift; remove at the first clinically possible moment.

## Scientific rationale

### Indications

- Acute urinary retention with pain or risk of kidney damage.
- Strict monitoring of urine output in critically ill patients.
- Management of incontinence in pressure ulcers or terminal care (according to institutional protocol).
- Urological or pelvic surgery according to prescription.

### Relative contraindications

- Suspected or confirmed urethral trauma.
- Unresolved urethral stricture.
- Ongoing procedure by urology (coordinate).

### Material

- Sterile urinary catheter kit (gauge according to prescription).
- Sterile water-soluble lubricating gel.
- Antiseptic (alcoholic chlorhexidine or povidone-iodine according to protocol).
- Sterile drape, sterile gloves.
- Closed drainage system and non-compressive securing device.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **SATI/SADI 2024 (Cornistein et al.):** Do not routinely place a catheter. Re-evaluate necessity daily and remove at the first clinically possible moment to prevent CAUTI.

> **SHEA/IDSA 2022 (Lo et al.):** Prevention bundle for catheter-associated urinary tract infections.

### Aftercare

- Maintain daily perineal hygiene with water and soap; do not routinely use antiseptics on the meatus.
- Monitor for obstruction, leaks, hematuria, suprapubic pain, or fever.
- Do not change the catheter or bag routinely; only in case of obstruction, disconnection, or institutional protocol.

### Complications — stop and notify

- Inability to pass the catheter or abundant urethral bleeding.
- Cloudy, purulent urine accompanied by fever (suspected CAUTI).
- Absence of urine output with abdominal pain (obstruction or malposition).

bibliography.citation: Cornistein W, Nuccetelli Y, Rodríguez VM, et al. Infección urinaria asociada a sonda vesical. Actualización y recomendaciones intersociedades 2024. Medicina (B Aires). 2025;85(2):1-15. (SATI / SADI).
Cornistein W, et al. Catheter associated urinary tract infection: Update and intersociety recommendations, 2024. Revista Argentina de Terapia Intensiva (SATI).
SATI — Critical Infectious Diseases Committee. Guidelines and consensus (CAUTI, HAI prevention).
Lo E, Nicolle LE, Coffin SE, et al. Strategies to Prevent Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infections in Acute Care Hospitals: 2022 Update. Infect Control Hosp Epidemiol. 2022.
